turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I thought every variant needed a name so the compiler could check it, but when I tried to use an anonymous value in my state machine, the borrow checker didn't complain at all. It feels like magic.

That's not magic; it's how zero-cost tokens are implemented using unit variants in your enum definition. When you define a variant with no fields, Rust treats that entire token as a single atomic value during compilation. Think of it like a physical switch on a circuit board: when the switch is flipped to one position, the state changes instantly without needing any intermediate calculations or memory writes.
Oh! So if I have a State enum with three unit variants, the compiler knows exactly which branch to take based on just that single bit of information stored in the enum itself?

Exactly. The type system encodes the control flow directly into the memory layout. Your State enum becomes a compile-time token machine where each unit variant represents a distinct state without carrying extra data. When you match on this enum, the compiler generates specialized code paths for each branch, effectively removing runtime overhead.
Wait, I thought I had to manually calculate the next state using some formula or function call every time? That's why my old implementation was so slow compared to this approach!

You were assuming you needed runtime computation for state transitions. But with unit variants, the transition logic is baked into the type itself. The compiler performs the branching at compile time using conditional compilation techniques, so your actual execution path is determined entirely by which variant is active.
So if I want to add a new state without changing my existing logic, I just add another unit variant and the compiler handles everything?

That's correct. Adding a new unit variant is like adding a new lane to your circuit board; it doesn't affect how traffic flows through the existing lanes until you actually route something into that new lane.
I see now. The real power here is that I don't need any runtime bookkeeping or extra variables to track which state I'm in, right?

Precisely. Your current type alone contains all the necessary information for the compiler to determine behavior at compile time. This eliminates the need for separate runtime tracking mechanisms or complex formulas.
I'm trying to port my old Go state machine, but I feel like Rust is doing something different. In Go I just switch on a string tag at runtime with an if statement inside the loop.

That's exactly why unit variants are so powerful here. When you define your state as an enum with only unit variants like Active or Idle, those values act as compile-time tokens instead of runtime strings. Your compiler knows the state at compile time and can optimize away dead code.
Wait, so I'm not actually running any logic? That sounds like magic to me. How do I even get that behavior without writing a bunch of if statements?

You don't write runtime checks anymore because the type system enforces it for you. You define your state machine using enums where each variant represents a distinct phase, and methods like next_state() or is_active() can be implemented on those types to return different behaviors based purely on the current enum value.
I'm still confused. It feels like I'm just moving the if logic into a trait and that doesn't solve my problem of having to manually manage state transitions in my existing codebase.

The key is letting the compiler do the work for you with traits. You can implement a Trait called StateTransitions on your enum so that methods like get_next_state() are resolved by the compiler based on which variant is active, not checked at runtime.

Okay, I see it now but I still think this approach will make my code harder to maintain since I have to implement these traits for every single state in my machine instead of just writing one switch statement.

Actually the opposite happens. Once you define your enum with unit variants and attach the logic to them via a trait, you only need to implement that Trait once per behavior pattern rather than duplicating conditional logic for every single state transition in your code.
I think I understand it now. So unit variants are basically like constants but with methods attached through traits?
Exactly they act as compile-time tokens carrying behavior through a trait, which is why this pattern is so useful for optimizing your type-state machines without runtime overhead.
